videos.title: The AI Bubble Burst 2026: EVERYTHING You Need To Know
videos.text: Peter Thiel has just dumped his entire Nvidia stake [music] along with 76% of his Tesla shares. Michael Burry, who shorted against the housing market during the 2008 financial crisis inspired movie The Big Short, currently has an entire portfolio made up of short positions in Nvidia and Palantir. Investors are spooked and they're wondering if the AI bubble is about to burst. Let me tell you a quick story about bubbles. The year is 1998. The internet is in its early days. New, exciting internet companies are emerging. Investors are pumping in money in a frenzy. People invest thousands and thousands and stock prices keep going up and up. What could go wrong? In early 2000, the dot-com bubble burst. The Nasdaq, which tracks the biggest US tech companies on the stock market, fell nearly 80%. Bill Gates lost 1.8 billion dollars in 1 week and 52% of internet companies went out of business. Investors weren't wrong in thinking that the internet was going to define the coming decades, but their overconfidence still created one of the biggest investment bubbles anyone had ever seen. Fast forward to present day and it looks like the same thing might be happening again. The AI bubble could be about to burst. Why do I think this? There are three big forces at play here. Let me take you through them one by one. One, the battle for AI dominance. Did you know that there are currently only seven companies propping up the stock market? They're known by investors as the Magnificent Seven. These seven companies are Microsoft, [music] Meta, Alphabet, who run Google, Amazon, Tesla, Apple, and Nvidia. These seven companies make up a whopping 37% of the entire S&P 500, which is the top 500 publicly traded companies in the US. These seven companies are all racing [music] to become the leader in AI, investing billions and billions to try to achieve that goal. These billions trickle down through the rest of the S&P 500 and the rest of the economy as a whole, being spent on anything from AI chip manufacture to large-scale infrastructure. Ever heard about Meta's plan to build nuclear power plants to power their AI data centers? That's just one example of this massive, interlinking AI economy. Next year, these companies are projected to spend a total of 330 billion dollars on AI, a staggering amount that's bigger than the GDP of many countries. So, is this the fourth industrial revolution or is it a bubble that's about to burst? You might have noticed that despite everyone talking about inflation and a cost of living crisis, the stock market is actually doing pretty well. That's because the AI spending from the Magnificent Seven companies is propping it up. In fact, without this spending, the US would probably already be in a recession, but is this going to carry on? Well, it's projected that by 2026, global AI spending will reach 500 billion dollars. The spending on power and resources for AI is expected to reach 3 trillion dollars per year by 2030. So, why are these companies willing to stake it all in AI? They're betting on an automation revolution. It's estimated that at least 60% of jobs will be affected by AI in the coming years, meaning that whoever controls the AI controls the totality of the economic power. It's almost like an arms race. But, here's the catch. It's not just companies competing in the AI arms race, it's countries as well. In 2017, Vladimir Putin famously said, "The one who becomes the lead in AI will be the ruler of the world." That's why all the world's superpowers and especially the US and China are competing to become the leaders in AI. There are two possible scenarios in the near future. Either AI will deliver on its promise and continue propping up the stock market as the technology becomes more and more ubiquitous or the technology won't develop as fast as expected and as companies and countries continue to pour in billions without seeing the financial reward they expect, investors could start to pull the plug. This brings us on to the second big force, the AI economy circular loop. You see, these companies are recording huge amounts of revenue, but all is not quite what it seems. The different companies in the AI space, Microsoft, Nvidia, other players like OpenAI and Intel, are all basically passing money between each other as part of a closed system. Here's how it works. If OpenAI gives Microsoft 58 billion dollars to use their data centers and Microsoft gives Nvidia the same amount to buy their chips and then Nvidia then invests that back into OpenAI, then each company can record 58 billion dollars in revenue despite not actually making a profit. But, their stock price rises regardless because they can technically say they've made 58 billion dollars in revenue. The true king of this closed system is Nvidia. Since ChatGPT launched in November 2022, their share price has rocketed up a colossal 1,600%. That's because they sell their AI chips to everyone in the market. They then invest this money back into the other companies. It's almost like paying your customers to buy your product and it could be creating artificial demand for its chips. That's why a company like OpenAI is trading at 40 times its revenue, having a valuation of 500 billion dollars and a revenue of just 12 billion dollars. Have you noticed that its product, ChatGPT, which has revolutionized the world, is free and its page doesn't feature any ads? How is OpenAI making enough money to justify its massive investments and ridiculous stock price? The game plan for these companies could be to try to get the world completely dependent on their AI products and then, once they're hooked, they start charging big prices. And that strategy could pay off big time. So, then, why would the bubble burst? Number three, the AI data wall. AI has been improving at an unbelievable speed. You only need to look at AI-generated videos from 1 or 2 years ago and compare them with now to see how massive these improvements have been. But, there's something coming up in the future that could put that progress to an end. It's called the data wall. Have you heard about data being new gold? Well, that's because AI is trained on data. Up till now, AI models have been trained on all the human-generated data that's available. So, that's every book, every piece of research, every article, every single thing that's ever been written or created and put on the internet. But, it's predicted that by 2027, it will have consumed every piece of human-generated data there is. That could see progress start to slow down and you guessed it, the bursting of the AI bubble. If progress stalls, investors could start pulling out their money. Scaling past the data wall is feasibly possible. It would be a question of transitioning AI models to start learning from synthetic data. Synthetic data is data that's artificially generated rather than collected from real-world events or people. It's created using algorithms, simulations, or machine learning models to mimic the patterns and statistical properties of real data. It's yet to be seen whether or not this will work. So, here's the trillion-dollar question. Is this a bubble? Well, I think it is. When I take into account the overvaluation of certain companies, the potential problems with scaling, the revenues not reflecting profits, and the general geopolitical landscape, I think this is likely a bubble that's going to burst. However, what I don't know is when the bubble will burst. And that's not to say that I don't think AI is a revolutionary technology. It almost certainly is. But, just like with the dot-com bubble, transformative technology does not equal immunity to bubbles. And just a reminder, I'm not a financial adviser and this is not financial advice. You should always do your own research >> [music] >> and come to your own conclusions. I would continue to invest. We could still be closer to the start of the bubble than to the end. And the prices could continue to rise astronomically in the coming years. By investing the same amount every month in low-cost index funds that track the overall market, over time, you will ride out any upturns or downturns and your investments will likely trend upwards over a long enough time span. History shows that the market always bounces back. So, as long as you don't try to be too clever and time the market, you're likely to be okay. Make sure your portfolio is well diversified. You don't want all your investments in one sector, region, or technology. If you spread your assets across stocks, bonds, gold, real estate, crypto, you should be able to ride out any downturns. Thanks for watching. If you have any questions, whack them in the comments and I'll aim to get back to you. See you on the next one.

videos.title: Here's why the AI stock bubble could burst in 2026
videos.text: Amazon, Google's parent company, Alphabet, Meta, and Microsoft poured 400 billion US into AI this year. Most of it towards data centers. >> I think what we're seeing in the market overall is this decision that maybe we were a little too optimistic, a little ahead of the curve of what AI can do so quickly. >> The AI wave has supercharged some of the biggest names in tech. The magnificent seven stocks are Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla. They have a combined value on the stock market of more than 21 trillion US. And as of October 2025, they made up nearly 40% of the S&P 500. >> Nvidia, who makes the chips that everybody's using, is making a lot of money. They're selling shovels in a gold rush. But the other companies like Open AI that are using those chips are not making that much money. In fact, they're losing money. >> There's a lot of questions about the monetization of AI and whether or not that is near-term going to happen. >> Gary Marcus is the author of Taming Silicon Valley. He has concerns about how powerful this small club of AI billionaires have become. >> It should remind you of the days of the railroad barons. Um, I mean, it's an extraordinary amount of concentration and power. >> A select club of tech companies with access to tons of capital, resources, and close connections to US President Donald Trump. Marcus worries a sustainable business model for many of these companies involves compromising users. I don't think that these companies can make a profit on this unless they turn to surveillance fundamentally. Unless they start selling deeply personal data like the conversations people have with chat GPT to governments to political campaigns and so forth, they're going to monetize you. You're going to completely give up your privacy and I find that to be terrifying. >> He sees the potential for a major correction in the AI space. >> It is a perfect storm. We have corporate irresponsibility. We have a government that's too close. We have assets that are wildly overvalued. Of course, it could all blow up in 2026. >> Though many have drawn comparisons to the bursting of the.com bubble, others like Marcus see something that more closely resembles the financial crisis with everyday people footing the bill. >> I'm worried that the banks are overleveraged, that the investors are overleveraged, and we're going to wind up with something like 2008. and Gaviola, Global News, Toronto.

videos.title: Big Tech Is Losing Billions: Is the AI Bubble Bursting? | Vantage with Palki Sharma
videos.text: For years, Big Tech could do no wrong. Stock swords exploded as AI hype exploded and investors chaired every announcement, but suddenly the same companies began losing billions of dollars. Let me show you the numbers. Since January this year, Big Tech has lost over $0.3 trillion in combined value of $1.3 trillion. Microsoft Is Down by About $63 Billion. Amazon is down by $343 billion. Apple is down by 256 billion. Alphabet is down by $88 billion and Video is also down by nearly $90 billion. Why is this happening? You See The AI ​​Race Is Not Cheap. It is one of the most expensive tech battles in history. Companies are pouring 100s of billions of dollars. They are pouring this money into data centers, chips and infrastructure. Amazon Plus to Boost Spending by More Than 50%, They Will Take It Up to $200 Billion Dollars. Alphabet may spend about $185 billion. And this is making investors nervous. Because where are the profits. There are many significant profits to show yet. So, investors are wary that all of this spending will pay off at all. Weather This Is an AI Bubble Just Waiting to Pop. And That Fear Is Not Unwarred. AI companies are consistently announcing billion dollar deals with each other, but the money seems to be going in circles. Tech Open AI Nvidia. For example, they announced a $100 billion deal. It's a mega deal $100 billion dollars. It was celebrated with a lot of pomp. But then it just vanished. All the Conversation Around the Deal Died Down. And suddenly, the deal wasn't happening at all. A $100 Billion Deal Just Disappeared Into Thin Air. Plus there's another problem here. The Biggest Threat Is Not AI. It Is What Powers AI. We're talking about memory chips. Memory chips are the backbone of every device. Your phones, laptops, servers, cars, everything needs memory chips and the world is running out of them. Top Tech Leaders Are Warning About a Global Shortage. The likes a feel on Musk and Team Cook. Both say that memory supply constraints could hit industries worldwide. The Shortage Is Centered Around The Drum. Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRM) This is a critical chip used in almost all electronics. Between December 2025 and January 2026, DRAM prices shot up by 80 to 90%. And why is this happening? Because AI data centers are following all these chips. Companies like Open AI and major cloud companies are buying millions of AI accelerators. These accelerators need memory chips which means AI servers get priority and consumers get left overs. Even chip suppliers are sounding alarms. They say that demand in this decade could be bigger than anything the industry has ever seen. Translation The Supply Can't Keep Up. So there is a clear demand and supply problem. The demand is soaring but the supply is limited. And this is affecting companies. Sony Group May Delay Its Next PlayStation Launch to 2028 and 2029 Even Nintendo May Raise Console Prices Even Lenovo Says the Imbalance Between Supply and Demand Is Not Temporary. The Shortage Can Last for Years. So, some tech firms are trying to avoid that. Like Tesla, Tesla is planning their own joint chip factory. But not everyone can build a chip factory. So, what does this trend mean for the rest of the world? Well first things first, AI is the future. No two ways about it. AI Is Here to Stay and Markets Do Not Crash Because Technology Fails. Markets Crash When Expectations Get Unrealistic. Right Now Investors Are Worried About Three Things. AI Spending Is Too High. Returns may take years and competition could shrink profits. That explains the falling stokes. Those Fierce. They Explain the Falling Stocks. Despite these fears, the chip demand continues to grow and it shows that AI is here to stay. So here is the bottom line. This is not the end of AI or Big Tech. This is the end of the easy money era. The Next Phase of the AI Race Won't Be About Promises Alone. It will be about profits. And that's a very hard game to play. Power did end quietly. Some Deaths Are Not Accidents, They Are Messages: A Gun Shot, a Crowd, a Country Frozen, the Killer Disappears: The Question: Don't This Is Not Just About Who Died, It's About What Followed: Governments Failed, Armies Moved, History Riot Itself, Five Leaders, Five Moments, One Pattern, The Assassination

videos.title: Here's What Happens When the AI Bubble Bursts
videos.text: Pure [ __ ] hell. That's what happens if the AI bubble bursts. I was working in tech when the dot bomb went off and started my career on Wall Street when the 2008 financial crisis kicked off. I've seen what happens when markets implode. And today I'm going to help you understand five things. One, what a hypothetical AI crash looks like. Two, why there probably will be a crash. Three, which stocks get hit hardest. Four, how to protect your portfolio if the AI bubble bursts. And five, how we plan to weather the storm. Let's get into it ASAP. Part one, what an AI crash looks like. So imagine this. A major hyperscaler reports slowing AI revenue growth and cuts future guidance, citing customer hesitation relating to ROI visibility. Something that's being brought up a lot, actually. That's just the first domino. In turn, NVIDIA issues a more soft, cautious chip demand outlook and memory makers follow suit. Neocloud start pulling back their aggressive guidance as companies begin cancelling contracts which, as it turns out, were easily cancellable in the first place. The circular finance deals start falling like dominoes, whilst Oracles credit default swaps go through the roof. AI related stocks and indices plunged 30 to 70% over a matter of weeks taking the entire market down with them. "Muh gains" have turned into molasses for most retail investors. Reads like a fiction novel, but it's a plausible scenario based on past market crashes. We also need to consider Rumsfeld unknown unknowns. AGI might get out of its sandbox and go rogue, starting to attack infrastructure across North America. Oh no, that's not AI. Actually, it's the North Koreans who are actually more technically savvy than you might think. Turns out they can do some very malicious things with artificial intelligence. Maybe another pandemic hits and that amplifies the shocks. We can "what if" until the cows come home, but maybe we should get on to part two. Why there will probably be a crash. This time it's different. Those are the four most dangerous words in investing. All disruptive technologies follow something called Gartner's Hype Cycle. This image was taken from their recent webinar on how AI is progressing through the hype cycle. I love how simple this is. It just shows you how hype quickly disconnects from maturity. Both those things are very intuitive, right? It takes time for technologies to mature and everybody gets really excited when something exciting comes along. It's a tale as old as time. Here you can see how they've segmented AI into various categories and then plotted them along various points of the hype cycle. The strong concentration of dots on the left here implies that nearly all AI technologies are still seeing increasing hype. That is before they start falling down the trough of disillusionment. Some AI technologies like generative AI have seen peak hype while cloud AI services are starting to show maturity. Here they're showing us how to compare the 2024 hype cycle to 2025 in order to show progression and even a departure to full maturity. Computer vision, a category we've covered for over a decade now, is now, they say, fully mature and commercialized. Will AI and its various manifestations be the first technology to pass through Gartner's hype cycle unfased? Probably not. Various types of technologies will be impacted in varying degrees of severity, as their presentation talks about. Now, it's becoming increasingly difficult to just ball this whole thing together and call it AI. But here are three additional reasons we believe that AI as a holistic technology is likely to see a bubble burst. One, the circular deal structure. I'm sure you've all seen this diagram being passed around. The convoluted relationships between all the key players out there. It just takes one domino. This is a very precarious situation. Two, funding ideas with billions of dollars sight unseen. I recall nine years ago when I first set out to interview AI startups across this globe. I spent two years doing that. At that time I was blown away that there were something like 1,300 AI startups. Well, now there are 70,000 operating globally. AI drives 70% of venture capital activity. And this is happening. Giving someone a billion dollars for their idea when they don't even have a product or a plan for a product is objectively ludicrous. Number three, the insane levels of subsidization. It truly blows my mind that anyone on this planet with an internet connection can access the world's most powerful technology for free. That's not going to last forever. That's taking up a lot of money to subsidize. So now is the time that you've been waiting for. One of the toughest questions ever hasn't been answered yet. Is the Strait of Hormuz open or closed? Not even our Romanian fortune teller who recently came out of retirement to do some one-off consulting work could answer that question. But here's an easier question to answer that you're all asking. What happens to my portfolio when the AI bubble bursts? Is your portfolio ready for a crisis? Probably not because you've been listening to all the instant analysts out there in social media talking about diversification and muh full port strat. "Muh full port strat." That brings us to part three. which stocks get hit hardest. Companies that go buy NVIDIA's GPUs, slap a big premium price tag on them, and then rent or sell them--they're not going to fare too well when demand starts to subside. Which is inevitably what's going to happen in the event of the AI bubble bursting. Neoclouds come to mind. Companies that didn't have a strong balance sheet before the AI boom, they're going to be in a bit of trouble because any decrease in demand is going to result in a reverse rerate. And companies with large debt loads to service that are relying on all these circular contracts--they're going to be in a world of hurt. That's because debt gets you in trouble more often than anything else. One can visualize a lot of these Neocloud providers getting bought up for pennies on the dollar by hyperscalers when the poop hits the propeller. And of course, the entire S&P 500 is going to take a big hit. That comment we made earlier about pure hell relates to the extent that the US is exposed to the AI trade. One Harvard economist attributed to 92% of US GDP growth in the first half of 2025 to investments in AI data centers and supporting technology. And guess what? When the US sneezes, the world catches a cold. Given that, we can then start to consider relative impact. So tech sector versus other sectors, growth versus value, momentum stocks, US stocks versus global stocks. People who talk about diversification show they have very little understanding of how wealth gets built predictably over time. Which brings us to part four, how to protect your portfolio. So first of all, you need to understand and quantify your exposure to AI today. Do you have any global equities exposure? What about other asset classes than equities? What's the maximum amount of your assets under management that's exposed to any single asset? What percentage of your portfolio is in technology? Bearing in mind that technology now accounts for 38% of the S&P 500. That's nuts. Steps you could take. Well, you could consider some exposure to what we call HALO assets. We covered that in a recent piece. You can have some value exposure alongside your growth exposure. You can consider two-birds-with-one-stone plays in that realm. So, you can have a value stock that's showing growth characteristics. You can also consider other asset classes. This is especially important if you're spending, not investing. And notice how everything I'm saying is still going to hold true regardless if the AI trade existed or not. And you also want to consider foreign equities, even though they've historically underperformed. And here's why. Today, the S&P 500 is more concentrated than it has been at any point in time since the late 1990s tech bubble. 10 largest stocks now make up nearly 36% of the 500 stock index. To put that into perspective, that number was about 25% during the dot bomb era and just 15% in 1980. If the AI bubble bursts, the S&P 500 is going to get absolutely walloped. But what about the S&P 500 Ex-Tech? That brings me to part five, which is how we're planning to weather this storm. Now, I recall back during terrible tariff Tuesday, lots of investors emailing me to say, "Oh, I'm going totally to cash." That was a terrible idea. For any number of reasons. The first being that you should never respond to market events by changing your strategy. And the people that did that really they were faced with three outcomes. Either they're still holding cash now and sure the big beautiful woman hasn't sang yet and the market could crater 50% tomorrow and they could be right. But it's very likely right now that person's not sleeping very well at night. Scenario two, they could have bought back in shortly afterwards. Kind of pointless, right? What- what sort of conviction do you have jumping in and out of the markets like that? And then scenario three, you know, they bought back later on. They're certainly kicking themselves. What happens the next time there's a crisis, right? What are they going to do now? They're indecisive. In all of these cases, these folks had no rules-based process. And a rules-based process is not if the market goes down xx%, I'm going to dump and go to cash. That's not how rules-based methodologies work. Our strategies have always been the same in regards to asset allocation, perhaps moving minimally over time, but our strategies stay the same. Here you can see our asset allocation plotted as of this month. And look at where the majority of our money is. That's in a value strategy called Quantigence, which actually has value and growth characteristics. But when you look at the sector breakdown in this portfolio we're holding, in our Quantigence DGI portfolio, there's just one tech name out of 30. And you might say, well, you're missing tech exposure. Well, that's the Nanalyze portfolio, right? So, we get our growth exposure from that. We sit on both sides of the growth-value spectrum. And you can see other asset classes here that provide some resilience in times of turmoil. Now, we have a cash allocation in the tech portfolio that we're actively working with. If the market crashed, we'd probably put all that cash to work. Income's also coming off the DGI portfolio as well. Those alternatives provide some diversification. But let's be fair here. Since we don't have any fixed income, we can expect a massive draw down, corresponding to the S&P 500 in the event of a market crash. If you're a retiree late on in your investing journey, you want to reconsider being all equities, for that reason. Now, the cool thing about Quantigence is that it's really Ex-Tech. Just two names in our universe of 80 core stocks are technology stocks. So, it ought to be quite resilient to any impact to the S&P 500. In our paper, we looked at how that strategy actually outperforms the S&P 500 in regards to sharp ratio as well, which means you're getting more bang for your buck, more return for the risk you're taking. Takeaways here. The most important thing to do: have a strategy that doesn't change or respond to market events. If you're actively investing, you keep doing that with perhaps some opportunistic shifting between asset classes or categories, in the event of a crisis. Maybe you're contributing a bit more to growth than value because a lot of quality names are trading at a discount. If you're somebody who's in retirement and you're actively spending your money right now, then you might shift asset classes if needed. That means that perhaps you're taking your money now from a different asset class that isn't so heavily discounted. The new 4% rule, which is where you take a variable amount every year, that's going to lead to less income in troubled times, as it should. Now, I know I've mentioned some of the hottest stocks going right now several times in this presentation, Neoclouds. We did a piece on some of the potential problems with the Neocloud trade. Give that a watch next. Thanks so much for taking the time to watch this today.
